class_schedule.topic: Puritan Origins: Bradford, Bradstreet, and the Literature of Settlement
learning_objectives: Analyze the Puritan plain style and its theological foundations
Identify the typological method of reading events as fulfillments of biblical patterns
Understand how early American literature constructs a national mythology of providential destiny
readings: Bradstreet, 'The Author to Her Book' and 'Here Follows Some Verses upon the Burning of Our House'
Norton Anthology of American Literature, Colonial period introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: American literature begins with the Puritans, who saw themselves as building a 'city upon a hill' — a new Israel in the American wilderness. Their writing established patterns of self-examination, providential thinking, and national exceptionalism that still shape American culture.
key_concepts: The plain style: clarity and directness as spiritual virtues, ornament as vanity
Typology: reading contemporary events as repetitions of biblical patterns
The jeremiad: a sermon form lamenting decline from original purity and calling for renewal
Bradstreet's domestic poetry: finding spiritual meaning in everyday loss and affection
American exceptionalism: the belief that America has a special, divinely ordained mission
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of Bradford's account of the Mayflower landing, analyzing how he uses biblical typology to transform a practical event into sacred history
setup_requirements: Norton Anthology of American Literature, Vol. A

review.value: A method of biblical interpretation in which Old Testament events are read as prefiguring New Testament fulfillments; Puritans extended this method to read their own experiences as repetitions of biblical patterns
A sermon form named after the prophet Jeremiah, lamenting the community's fall from original virtue and calling for repentance and renewal — a recurring pattern in American public rhetoric

class_schedule.topic: The American Renaissance: Emerson and Transcendentalism
learning_objectives: Explain the core principles of Transcendentalism: self-reliance, intuition, and the Over-Soul
Analyze Emerson's essay style: aphoristic, digressive, and performance-based
Understand Transcendentalism as both a philosophical movement and a declaration of literary independence from Europe

lecture_notes.introduction: In the 1830s and 1840s, a group of New England intellectuals declared American cultural independence from Europe. Emerson was their prophet. His call for self-reliance, originality, and trust in individual intuition over inherited authority became the philosophical foundation of American literary identity.
key_concepts: Self-reliance: trust your own thought, because 'to believe your own thought, to believe that what is true for you in your private heart is true for all men — that is genius'
The Over-Soul: a universal spirit connecting all individual souls to each other and to nature
The American Scholar: Emerson's call for intellectual independence from European models
Transcendentalist epistemology: intuition as a higher faculty than reason or sensory evidence
The essay as performance: Emerson's sentences as standalone aphorisms rather than linear arguments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of key passages from 'Self-Reliance,' examining how Emerson's aphoristic style enacts the independence he advocates — each sentence stands alone, refusing to submit to the authority of the previous one
setup_requirements: Complete text of 'Self-Reliance' and 'The American Scholar'

review.value: Emerson's central doctrine: that conformity is the enemy of greatness, that society conspires against the individual, and that trust in one's own perception is the beginning of all authentic thought and action

Emerson's 1837 address declaring that American intellectuals must stop imitating European models and develop original thought grounded in American experience — called 'our intellectual Declaration of Independence' by Oliver Wendell Holmes

class_schedule.topic: Hawthorne and Melville: The Dark Romantics
learning_objectives: Analyze how Hawthorne uses allegory and symbolism to explore Puritan guilt and moral ambiguity
Understand Melville's engagement with the problem of evil and the limits of knowledge
Compare the Dark Romantics' skepticism with Emersonian optimism

lecture_notes.introduction: Hawthorne and Melville are the shadow side of the American Renaissance. Where Emerson proclaimed self-reliance and universal benevolence, Hawthorne and Melville explored guilt, doubt, obsession, and the terrifying possibility that the universe is indifferent or unknowable.
key_concepts: Hawthorne's ambiguity: is the forest meeting real or a dream? The text refuses to decide.
Allegory vs. symbolism: Hawthorne's symbols resist single interpretations
Melville's Bartleby as a figure of radical refusal: 'I would prefer not to'
The problem of charity: the narrator's inability to comprehend or help Bartleby
Dark Romanticism: exploring the human capacity for evil that Transcendentalism ignores
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'Young Goodman Brown,' focusing on the moments where Hawthorne deliberately refuses to clarify whether the forest events are real or imagined, and why that ambiguity is the story's point
setup_requirements: Printed copies of both assigned stories
Comparison chart: Transcendentalism vs. Dark Romanticism

review.value: Hawthorne's deliberate refusal to resolve his narratives into clear moral lessons, leaving the reader suspended between possible interpretations — a critique of the Puritan certainty he inherited

'I would prefer not to' — a phrase that is neither defiant nor submissive, that cannot be argued with, punished into compliance, or understood, and that exposes the limits of institutional and personal charity

learning_objectives: Analyze Whitman's free verse as a democratic formal principle: inclusive, expansive, and cataloguing
Analyze Dickinson's compression: how hymn meter, slant rhyme, and dashes create intensity
Understand how both poets reinvented the possibilities of American poetry in opposite directions
readings: Dickinson, Poems 249 ('Wild Nights'), 320 ('There's a certain Slant of light'), 465 ('I heard a Fly buzz — when I died')

lecture_notes.introduction: Whitman and Dickinson are the twin poles of American poetry. Whitman sprawls: his lines stretch to contain multitudes, cataloguing the entire nation. Dickinson compresses: her poems are explosions in small rooms, packing enormous meaning into hymn-meter quatrains. Together they define the range of what American poetry can do.
key_concepts: Whitman's catalogue technique: democratic inclusion through lists that place the president and the prostitute side by side
Free verse as political form: Whitman's rejection of inherited meters as a rejection of inherited hierarchies
Dickinson's hymn meter (common meter): the iambic tetrameter/trimeter alternation of Protestant hymns
Dickinson's dashes: creating pauses, alternatives, and syntactic ambiguity
Slant rhyme: Dickinson's characteristic near-rhymes that create unease and resist closure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side reading of Whitman's Section 6 of 'Song of Myself' and Dickinson's 'I heard a Fly buzz,' both addressing death but through radically different formal means
setup_requirements: Printed copies of all assigned poems
Prosody comparison handout: Whitman vs. Dickinson

review.value: Long lists of people, places, and activities that enact democratic inclusiveness formally — the line expands to contain everything, refusing to privilege or exclude

Near-rhymes (such as 'seen'/'stone' or 'Room'/'Storm') that create a sense of things almost but not quite fitting together, mirroring the poems' engagement with uncertainty and the limits of knowledge

class_schedule.topic: Slave Narratives and the Literature of Resistance
learning_objectives: Analyze the slave narrative as a literary genre with specific conventions and rhetorical strategies
Understand how Douglass constructs a public self through strategic self-representation
Identify the tension between individual achievement and collective liberation in abolitionist writing
readings: Douglass, Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass (Chapters 1-7)
Jacobs, Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl (selections)

lecture_notes.introduction: The slave narrative is the most important American literary genre that has no European equivalent. Born from the abolitionist movement, these narratives had to simultaneously prove the author's humanity, persuade white readers to oppose slavery, and navigate the conventions expected by a predominantly white audience.
key_concepts: Generic conventions: authenticating documents, apology for writing ability, journey from bondage to freedom
Douglass's literacy narrative: learning to read as both personal liberation and political weapon
The chiasmus: 'You have seen how a man was made a slave; you shall see how a slave was made a man'
Jacobs's gendered revision: how Incidents addresses sexual exploitation and challenges the conventions of sentimental fiction
The double audience: writing for sympathetic whites while maintaining dignity and agency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of Douglass's account of learning to read, analyzing how he structures the episode as a conversion narrative in which literacy replaces religious grace as the agent of transformation

review.value: Prefaces, letters, and testimonials by white abolitionists that framed slave narratives — necessary for publication but troubling in their implication that Black testimony required white validation
Douglass presents learning to read as the turning point in his transformation from slave to free man, inverting his master's claim that literacy would make a slave unfit for slavery

questions.question: What is typology, and how does Bradford use it in Of Plymouth Plantation to give the Pilgrims' experience spiritual significance?
questions.answer: Typology is the practice of reading events as fulfillments of biblical patterns. Bradford presents the Pilgrims' Atlantic crossing as a repetition of the Israelites' passage through the Red Sea, and their arrival in America as a new entry into the Promised Land. This transforms a practical settlement venture into sacred history with providential meaning.

rubric.key: Explains how typology creates spiritual significance

questions.question: According to Emerson in 'Self-Reliance,' why is conformity the enemy of genius? Cite a specific passage to support your answer.
questions.answer: Emerson argues that conformity forces individuals to abandon their own perception in favor of received opinion, which suppresses original thought. 'Whoso would be a man must be a nonconformist' — and 'A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds.' Conformity prevents individuals from trusting their own insight, which Emerson sees as the source of all genuine contribution to thought and culture.

rubric.key: Connects to Emerson's broader philosophical framework

questions.question: How does Hawthorne's 'Young Goodman Brown' deliberately maintain ambiguity about whether the forest events are real? Why is this ambiguity important to the story's meaning?
questions.answer: Hawthorne uses phrases like 'had Goodman Brown fallen asleep in the forest and only dreamed a wild dream?' while never confirming or denying it. The ambiguity is essential because whether the events are real or dreamed does not matter — what matters is that Brown can no longer trust his community. The story argues that the loss of faith, once experienced, is irreversible regardless of its cause.

rubric.key: Identifies specific textual strategies maintaining ambiguity
Explains why the ambiguity is thematically important
Connects to the story's argument about faith and trust

questions.question: Compare Whitman's and Dickinson's approaches to form. How does each poet's formal choices reflect their vision of the self and the world?
questions.answer: Whitman's sprawling free verse, long lines, and catalogues enact a democratic, expansive vision: the self contains multitudes and embraces everything. Dickinson's compressed hymn meter, slant rhyme, and dashes reflect a vision of the self as intense, interior, and confronting limits — knowledge is partial, certainty is elusive, and meaning must be wrung from small spaces.

rubric.key: Accurately describes each poet's formal characteristics
Connects form to vision of self and world for each poet
Comparison illuminates both poets rather than just listing differences

major_assignments.title: American Literary Identity Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,000-2,500 word essay analyzing how one or two assigned texts construct a distinctly American literary identity in relation to (or against) inherited European traditions. Integrate at least two secondary scholarly sources.
objectives: Analyze how American literature defines itself in relation to European precedents
Construct an argument about American literary identity grounded in close reading
Integrate scholarly sources that inform the historical and cultural context
Write clear, thesis-driven academic prose in MLA format
deliverables: Works Cited page with primary text(s) and at least 2 scholarly sources

criteria: Thesis makes a specific, arguable claim about American literary identity
Argument engages with the relationship between American and European traditions
Analysis avoids oversimplification of either national tradition
Conclusion extends the argument beyond mere summary
Close reading demonstrates careful attention to language and form
Textual evidence is well-chosen and directly supports the argument
Analysis connects formal features to cultural and historical context
Secondary sources support rather than replace the student's analysis
Prose is clear, precise, and free of errors
Essay is well-organized with effective transitions
Length requirements are met without padding
Strong argument about American literary identity with specific textual grounding
Detailed close reading connecting form to cultural context
Clear, polished academic prose in correct MLA format

literary_identity_essay.description: Essay on the construction of American literary identity

reading_responses.description: Weekly close reading responses to assigned texts

quizzes.description: Quizzes on literary history, terminology, and assigned readings

midterm_examination.description: Passage identification and analysis covering colonial through Transcendentalist periods

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with passage analysis and comparative essay

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Essays penalized 5% per day late. Reading responses not accepted late. Extensions require 48 hours advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work must be original individual work. Discussion is encouraged; written submissions are independent.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, including AI-generated text submitted as your own, will result in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Norton Anthology of American Literature (Vol. A), word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
